This figure shows the value of aggregate trading volume (in logs) on the New York Stock Exchange on October 3, 1995, the day the verdict of O.J. Simpson's murder trial was announced. The top, middle, and bottom panels display trading volume for, respectively, all, small, and large trades. Trades are sorted into five size groups, where small (large) trades are those in the bottom (top) quintile. The horizontal axis labels five-minute intervals starting from 9:30 a.m. EST. The vertical line marks the announcement time (10 a.m. PST or 1 p.m. EST). The solid horizontal line indicates the average (log) trading volume during that day (excluding the time period from 10:00 to 10:10 a.m.) for the trade size category displayed in the panel. The dashed horizontal line indicates the 5% confidence bound (1.96 times the standard deviation of (log) trading volume during the day). Data for this figure come from Trades and Quotes (TAQ). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
To create a sample of such distraction events, we use a variable (introduced by Eisensee and Strömberg (2007)) called news pressure. This variable measures the median number of minutes that U.S. news broadcasts devote to the first three news segments. For example, the O.J. Simpson trial verdict received 16.5 minutes of air time, the highest value for that year. Each year, we sort days into news pressure deciles and identify days belonging to the highest decile. We then parse through the headlines of news segments covered in the broadcasts and retain only those days for which the sensational news event is plausibly exogenous to the economy. Examples of such distracting news include the O.J. Simpson trial verdict, the Cessna plane crashing on the White House lawn, and the Challenger space shuttle explosion. Data on TV viewership confirm that these events drew the attention of U.S. households. Our final sample contains 551 days (distraction events) over the period from 1968 to 2014.
We begin by examining whether these news stories actually divert noise traders’ attention away from the stock market. Detailed trading records of households (from a large discount broker) and institutions (from Abel Noser Solutions, or ANcerno) show that trading volume drops on distraction days by 6.5% and 4%, respectively, before reverting to normal on the following day. We next construct several proxies for investor “biasedness,” in particular, the extent to which investors make losses from trading, churn their portfolios, underdiversify, and trade stocks covered in the media, and, for households, their gender and whether they were quick to adopt online trading. These factors are all related to the tendency of traders to be sensation-seeking, overconfident, or susceptible to media coverage. We find that, among both households and institutions, more “biased” investors are more prone to distraction, which confirms that our sensational news episodes primarily distract noise traders. We therefore exploit these episodes to study how short-lived changes to noise trading affect financial markets.
We next examine how the stock market behaves on sensational news days (i.e., when noise traders are inattentive). Although we find weak results for the overall market, we uncover pronounced effects when focusing on subgroups of stocks with high retail ownership—stocks in which noise trading is expected to be more pronounced (e.g., Lee, Shleifer, and Thaler (1991)). We confirm that trading activity declines (by about 3%) in the bottom tercile of stocks in terms of firm size, stock price, and institutional ownership—three variables that are negatively correlated with retail ownership. Exploiting CRSP daily data as well as Trades and Quotes (TAQ) intraday data, we then report that, on distraction days, liquidity worsens by 1% to 6%, volatility falls by 3%, and price reversals are reduced by 4%. These effects are statistically significant and robust across proxies and test methodologies (i.e., parametric versus nonparametric). Moreover, they are concentrated in stocks with high levels of retail ownership and dissipate monotonically in the other terciles. Cross-sectional tests confirm that distraction events that see a greater decline in trading also exhibit a more pronounced reduction in liquidity, volatility, and reversals, thus establishing that these effects are related. To strengthen the causal interpretation of our results, we discuss, and ultimately dismiss, endogeneity concerns related to confounding effects from economic news and reverse causality, as well as alternative explanations based on distracting agents other than noise traders and investor sentiment.1
To summarize, we find that distraction days are associated with reduced liquidity, volatility, and price reversals among stocks owned predominantly by individual investors. Using a simple extension of the Kyle (1985) model, we demonstrate that these findings can be attributed to a common shock—a reduction in noise trading. Indeed, when noise traders exit, market makers face worse adverse selection because they are at greater risk of being “picked off” by informed speculators, and hence liquidity drops. To the extent that inventory risk is priced, the reduction in noise trading also reduces volatility and price reversals.2
Our findings on liquidity contrast with those of Foucault, Sraer, and Thesmar (2011), who report an improvement in liquidity following a reform that discouraged retail (noise) trading on the French stock exchange. They explain their findings by noting that because the reform permanently dampened noise trading and in turn volatility, it reduced the inventory risk borne by market makers, that is, the risk associated with the future unwinding of their inventory. We rationalize the difference in results by noting that our noise shocks are transitory and therefore have a negligible effect on inventory risk. Instead noise trading has an immediate effect on adverse selection risk, which implies that a reduction in noise trading decreases liquidity, as we find. Put differently, isolating the effect of noise trading on adverse selection requires fluctuations in noise trading that are sufficiently short lived to not affect future volatility. We argue that the short-lived distraction shocks studied here are ideally suited for this purpose.
In our next set of tests, we examine how distraction effects have changed over time. We analyze the data separately over three subperiods, 1968–1992, 1993–2006, and 2007–2014. The second subperiod corresponds to the advent of the Internet, and the third to the rise of algorithmic traders—particularly high-frequency traders—who have come to dominate trading. We find that as we move from the first to the second period, the impact of distraction (among small stocks) is considerably greater for virtually all variables. As we proceed to the third period, the effect is further amplified for trading activity, volatility, and price reversals, but, surprisingly, it vanishes for liquidity.
Follow-up analyses suggest that these time series patterns are linked to the development of the Internet in the 1990s, and to the rise of algorithmic trading post-2007. Regarding the Internet, we document that, over the 1993–2006 period, distraction effects for all variables are more pronounced among tech stocks, which are a natural habitat for tech-savvy investors (who were early adopters of online information gathering and trading platforms). These results are consistent with prior research indicating that, by creating illusions of knowledge (by facilitating access to information) and control (by enabling online trading), the Internet increases investor overconfidence (Barber and Odean (2001b,2002)). An alternative interpretation is that the Internet causes an acceleration of news, which makes trading decisions less drawn out over time and hence distraction effects more easily identifiable.
To evaluate the link between algorithms and the diverging trends we document in the post-2007 period, we sort stocks based on proxies for the intensity of algorithmic trading. For stocks with more algorithmic trading, we find that distraction effects for trading activity, volatility, and price reversals are more pronounced, whereas the effect for liquidity is attenuated, in line with our time series evidence. We speculate that these patterns are explained by both technological advances—including improvements in hardware (e.g., computing power, custom-designed chips, and ultra-fast communication lines) and software (e.g., pattern recognition algorithms, “Big Data,” and artificial intelligence)—and new business practices (e.g., co-location, access to exchanges’ proprietary data feeds) that have made it easier to detect and anticipate noise trades. These changes may have allowed informed algorithmic traders to improve the timing of their trades (i.e., to trade when noise trading surges) and, perhaps more controversially, high-frequency traders to take advantage of noise traders by front-running their orders.3
The rest of this paper is organized as follows. SectionIdiscusses our contributions to the literature. SectionIIreviews our data and methodology, and SectionIIIconsiders the effect of distraction on retail and institutional investors. In SectionIV, we study how distraction shocks affect the stock market, and in particular, the stocks held predominantly by noise traders. SectionVpresents results of robustness checks and discusses endogeneity issues, and in SectionVIwe investigate how distraction effects have changed over time. SectionVIIconcludes. The Internet Appendix presents details on our model along with additional results.4
article.I. Related Literature and Contribution:
Our paper contributes to four strands of research. First, it adds to the growing literature on limited attention in financial markets. Prior work focuses largely on informed traders and the speed with which they impound public information into prices (see, e.g., Cohen and Frazzini (2008), DellaVigna and Pollet (2009), Hirshleifer, Lim, and Teoh (2009)) and, to a lesser extent, on market makers (Corwin and Coughenour (2008), Chakrabarty and Moulton (2012)). Our main contribution is to study noise traders—specifically, how their trading contributes to liquidity, volatility, and price efficiency.5We make an important methodological contribution by showing that distracting events can be exploited to study the causal effects of short-term fluctuations in noise traders’ attention. More broadly, our findings suggest that the impact of attention ultimately depends on who is (in)attentive (see also Ben-Rephael, Da, and Israelsen (2017)).
Second, our paper contributes to the literature on the determinants of liquidity. Microstructure theory has identified two opposing channels through which noise traders affect liquidity. On the one hand, adverse selection models (e.g., Glosten and Milgrom (1985), Kyle (1985)) demonstrate that insiders exploit noise trades to conceal their informed trades. When noise trading lessens, market makers face more risk of being adversely selected and seek compensation by increasing spreads and/or price impact. On the other hand, inventory risk models (e.g., Ho and Stoll (1981), Grossman and Miller (1988)) emphasize that market makers are concerned about fluctuations in their inventory's value, as might be triggered by future noise trading shocks. From this perspective, a reduction in noise trading improves liquidity. How liquidity is ultimately shaped by noise trading therefore remains an open question—and one that is hard to answer empirically because noise trading is endogenous and could itself be a function of liquidity.6
To date, the evidence has relied on structural estimations (e.g., the “probability of informed trading (PIN) model” of Easley, Kiefer, O'Hara, and Paperman (1996a)) or laboratory experiments (e.g., Bloomfield, O'Hara, and Saar (2009)). Only two papers offer causal evidence drawn from quasi-natural experiments, namely, Greene and Smart (1999) and Foucault, Sraer, and Thesmar (2011). These papers exploit changes in noise trading triggered by, respectively, analyst recommendations published in the press and a stock market reform. But because these experiments involve long-lasting changes to noise trading, their findings on liquidity accentuate the inventory risk channel. This is striking in Foucault, Sraer, and Thesmar (2011), where the reduction in the intensity of noise trading is permanent and hence the reduction in inventory cost is so large that it swamps any adverse selection effect and thus results in a liquidity improvement.7In contrast, our single-day decline in the intensity of noise trading leads to no detectable reduction in inventory cost but does lead to reduced liquidity because adverse selection now dominates.8Overall, our findings highlight the importance of the adverse selection channel, the bedrock of a large literature on trading under asymmetric information, and help reconcile prior conflicting evidence by emphasizing that the duration of noise trading shocks varies significantly across settings.
Our third contribution is to describe how attention interacts with behavioral biases, and in doing so offer a more nuanced view of attention—rather than being invariably good, attention can be bad for investors who trade poorly. Indeed, we find that more “biased” (e.g., unskilled, overconfident, sensation-seeking) investors are more prone to distraction, and thus, to the extent that their trading profits are insufficient to cover their trading costs (Odean (1999), Barber and Odean (2000)), their performance will actually improve by watching TV instead of trading.
Finally, by studying the effect of distraction on different measures of trading activity, we shed light on the role that attention plays in the decision-making process of a retail trader (data limitations prevent us from drawing clear-cut conclusions for institutional traders). More specifically, for retail investors, we document that distraction has a strong negative effect on the extensive margin (i.e., whether or not to trade) but not on the intensive margin (i.e., how much to trade). These findings are difficult to reconcile with models in which investors gradually curb their trading intensity as they pay less attention (Peng and Xiong (2006), Van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp (2010)), but are consistent with models that assume a fixed attention cost for accessing the stock market (Merton (1987), Abel, Eberly, and Panageas (2007), Chien, Cole, and Lustig (2012), Abel, Eberly, and Panageas (2013)).
article.II. Data and Methodology:
We identify our candidate events using thenews pressuremeasure of Eisensee and Strömberg (2007). News pressure is defined as the median number of minutes that U.S. news broadcasts devote to the first three news segments. The authors argue that this variable is a good indicator of the extent of newsworthy material available on a given day. “For instance, on October 3, 1995, a jury found O.J. Simpson not guilty of two counts of murder. That night, ABC, CBS, and NBC devoted all of their first three news segments to that story. The top three news segments comprised an average of sixteen minutes and thirty seconds—the highest value of that year” (Eisensee and Strömberg (2007, p. 207)).9Figure2provides a time series plot of daily news pressure over the sample period. Daily news pressure oscillates around a mean of eight minutes with occasional spikes of 10 minutes or more.
Daily news pressure and distraction events. The blue line shows daily news pressure over the period 1968–2014. The red dots mark a subset of the distraction events that we use in this paper. Specifically, they consist of days when news pressure is the highest in a given year and that have survived our filter for excluding potential economic news (see SectionII). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
We focus on the spikes in daily news pressure. In particular, for each sample year, we select the 10% of business days with the highest news pressure to identify our candidate events. This procedure yields an initial list of 1,084 event-days. One might object that news pressure could be high because news broadcasts cover important economic news, which, rather than distract attention, actually draw attention to the stock market. We dismiss this concern on three grounds.
First, economic news would bias our resultsagainstfinding a distraction effect. Indeed, a large literature documents that stock returns are orders of magnitude more variable on days with economic news (see, e.g., Cutler, Poterba, and Summers (1989), Ederington and Lee (1993)). In contrast, we expect (and find) less return volatility under our distraction hypothesis. Similarly, public news typically coincides with large increases in trading activity, which are attributed to increases in disagreement and/or differences in information processing speeds among market participants (Hong and Stein (2007), Foucault, Hombert, and Roşu (2016)). As with volatility, we expect (and find) the opposite effect under distraction. Moreover, the impact of economic news should be felt most strongly among large stocks (which are known to lead small stocks in terms of price discovery; see, e.g., Lo and MacKinlay (1990), McQueen, Pinegar, and Thorley (1996), Hou and Moskowitz (2005)), whereas our distraction effects primarily affect small stocks.
Second, correlation analysis indicates that macroeconomic news releases, business activity indicators, and investor sentiment together explain only a small fraction of the variation in news pressure (see Internet AppendixIII.B). It therefore appears that news pressure is driven by sensational stories that are mostly orthogonal to news about future cash flows and discount rates (i.e., economically important news).
Finally, we exclude high-news pressure days on which the headlines of any of our selected broadcasters’ top three news segments contain at least one word from a list of economic keywords.10We are left with a list of 551 event-days from 1968 to 2014 that we are confident in classifying as both noneconomic and potentially distracting. The corresponding events comprise the sample ofdistraction eventsused in our analyses.
Panel B of TableIpresents a partial list of these events along with a short description of the day's major news headline. In particular, the panel lists the top two distraction events by news pressure for each year. The stories in this list involve accidents (e.g., Challenger explosion and Minneapolis bridge collapse), terrorist attacks (e.g., Lockerbie plane bombing, Oklahoma City bombing, and London bombing),11assassination attempts (e.g., on President Reagan and on Pope John Paul II), shootings (e.g., Littleton school shooting, Virginia Tech massacre, and Tucson shooting), criminal court rulings (e.g., O.J. Simpson, John DeLorean, and William Calley), celebrity deaths (e.g., Lady Diana and Michael Jackson), military skirmishes (e.g., Grenada invasion, USS Stark incident, and Iraq Fallujah uprising), natural disasters (e.g., Haiti earthquake and Oklahoma tornado), and political scandals (e.g., Watergate hearings and Iran-Contra scandal). In essence, our list spans news stories that captured national headlines but had an arguably negligible effect on the U.S. economy. These stories differ markedly from the type of news that has been shown to distractrationalinvestors, such as other firms’ corporate announcements or trading activity (Corwin and Coughenour (2008), Hirshleifer, Lim, and Teoh (2009), Chakrabarty and Moulton (2012)). Robustness tests reported in SectionVshow that our results are not sensitive to the inclusion (or exclusion) of any particular event or to the specific keywords employed as filters. SectionValso offers a detailed discussion of potential endogeneity concerns.
An important prerequisite for the distraction hypothesis is that our events absorb the attention of potential investors (and thereby reduce the attention available for trading in the stock market).12We directly test the first part of this statement using television viewership data purchased from Nielsen Research for the 1992–2014 subperiod. More specifically, we conduct an event study to determine whether television viewership during the 6:30 p.m. to 7:00 p.m. news broadcasts by ABC, CBS, and NBC (from which the news pressure variable is calculated) increases on distraction days.13To ensure that any viewership increase is not confined to after-trading hours, we complement this measure with the average daily viewership of CNN, a dedicated news channel. Panel A of TableIreports the results. The viewership for CNN (column (1)) and for other broadcasts’ news programing (column (2)) is, respectively, 33% and 3% higher on distraction days—an effect that is both economically and statistically highly significant.14We therefore find strong evidence that U.S. residents are “glued to the TV” on distraction days. We next describe our other data sources and explain our econometric methodology.
We employ three different data sets or trades to study the effect of distraction events on traders. The first source comprises about 1.9 million common stock trades executed by 78,000 households at a large discount brokerage house between January 1991 and November 1996 (for details, see Barber and Odean (2000)). Our sample contains 66 distraction events occur during that period. Our second source comprises data on institutional trades from Abel Noser Solutions, commonly known as ANcerno.15These data cover institutional stock transactions for 835 asset managers from 1999 to mid-2011, a period for which we have 99 distraction events. Although this data set does not allow us to track individual funds, it includes an identifier for the management company of the fund executing the trade, which enables us to follow management companies over time. The advantage of these two disaggregated data sets is that they allow us to determine which investors are more prone to distraction.
Our third data source on trades consists of aggregated transactions in all NYSE/AMEX/Nasdaq stocks. This information comes from the Institute for the Study of Security Markets (ISSM) and TAQ databases.16Although these data sets do not reveal traders’ identities, they do allow us to distinguish between small and large trades.17Small trades were an effective proxy for retail trading until the early 2000s, when order splitting by institutions became popular (Lee and Radhakrishna (2000), Barber, Odean, and Zhu (2009), Hvidkjaer (2008)). For this reason, we limit our analysis of aggregated transaction data to the period 1991–2000, during which 105 distraction events occurred.
We obtain stock market data from two sources. First, we use daily data from CRSP covering the period 1968–2014 (551 distraction events). Second, we again employ TAQ data for the period 1993–2014 (225 distraction events). We apply the filters and adjustments described by Holden and Jacobsen (2014) to address withdrawn or canceled quotes, and we use their interpolated time technique to improve the accuracy of liquidity measures.18All TAQ trades are signed using the Lee and Ready's (1991) algorithm. Throughout our analyses, we focus on common stocks (share codes 10 or 11) and exclude penny stocks (closing price < $1). We describe our stock market variables in SectionIV, where we also present results for the market wide analysis.
We start by purging any seasonal effects from all time series variables (e.g., stock market data, brokerage trading volume, and TV viewership) by regressing them on a set of dummy variables for years, calendar months, and days of the week (where the latter two are allowed to vary by year). We carry out our analyses on the residuals from these regressions, which ensures that the results are neither driven nor confounded by seasonal patterns.
Our estimation window includes both the pre- and the post-event periods—to neutralize any trend in the data—although results are unchanged if the window includes only the pre-event period. For variables related to stock market outcomes, we first calculate abnormalXat the stock level and then calculate the equal-weighted average over the portfolio of interest (e.g., the entire market or stocks in the bottom tercile of institutional ownership) for each event date. We test for the significance of abnormalXacross events using the parametric Boehmer, Musumeci, and Poulsen's (1991)t-test, or BMP for short, as well as a nonparametric rank test.20
article.III. Distraction and Noise Trading:
In this section, we study the effect of distraction on trading activity. In addition to setting the stage for the market wide investigation to follow, this analysis is valuable in its own right: Because investors can be distracted only if they were attentive to begin with, this analysis sheds light on investors’ decision-making process. In particular, we compare distraction effects across different measures of trading activity to identify which stages in the decision-making process are most sensitive to attention constraints. Our results thus inform the development of a positive theory of attention allocation.
We construct four measures of trading activity—which pertain to buys, sells, and their sum—for the retail and institutional data sets. The first measure, log($volume), is obtained by first aggregating the dollar value of all transactions on a given day and then taking logarithms. The remaining measures capture components of this aggregate measure. Our second measure, log(trade size), is the average (log) trade size, defined as the equal-weighted average per investor and stock, conditional on trading, of the logarithm of the trade value in dollars. The third measure, log(#stocks), is the average number of distinct stocks traded, defined as the equal-weighted average of the logarithm of the number of different stocks traded on a given day by an investor with at least one trade on that day. Our final measure, log(#investors), is the logarithm of the number of investors trading on a given day.
The last three measures above are intended to reflect different stages in an investor's decision-making process. The variable log(#investors) captures the decision of whether or not to trade (i.e., the extensive margin). A drop in this variable on distraction days would indicate that the process of directing attention to the stock market and transacting—which involves logging into one's brokerage account or calling up a broker—requires a fixed amount of attention, as in the models of Merton (1987), Abel, Eberly, and Panageas (2007,2013), and Chien, Cole, and Lustig (2012). The log(#stocks) variable reflects how much more attention is required tosearch forand trade additional stocks conditional on having traded at least once on that day. Past research suggests that, for underdiversified and short-sale–constrained retail investors, buys require more attention than sells during this phase because their choice set for buys is much larger than that for sells (Barber and Odean (2008)). Finally, models of rational attention predict that investors trade less aggressively when the information they possess is less precise (e.g., Verrecchia (1982), Peng and Xiong (2006), Van Nieuwerburgh and Veldkamp (2010)), as would be the case if investors were distracted. We therefore expect distraction days to be associated with a reduction in log(trade size), the average trade size conditional on trading on a given day.
Panel A of TableIIreports results using our retail brokerage data, for which there are 66 distraction events.21Dollar trading volume (log($volume)) is approximately 6.5% lower (p< 0.05) on distraction days, almost symmetrically across buys and sells. Breaking up this effect, we find a statistically significant decrease in all three components of volume. However, the observed effect on the intensive margin is considerably weaker than that on the extensive margin: average trade size and the number of distinct stocks traded both decrease by less than 2%, whereas the number of trading retail investors falls by 5%.22Taken together, these findings are more consistent with models assuming a fixed attention cost for accessing the stock market than with models in which distraction reduces the (perceived) precision of retail investors’ information. We also note that, in contrast to the other measures, the reduction in log(#stocks) is significantly greater for buys than for sells, which supports Barber and Odean's (2008) argument that retail buy decisions require more attention than sell decisions, due to the larger choice set for the former.
Panel B of TableIIreports results of a similar analysis conducted on institutional trades.23Again we find a significant drop in trading volume (p< 0.05), but its magnitude (about 4%) is smaller than for retail investors. Breaking up this effect yields results that are more nuanced than those for retail investors: Although all three components exhibit negative estimates, none is consistently significant. In particular, the evidence at the extensive margin (i.e., for log(#investors)) is less compelling for institutional than for retail investors. This might be explained by professional investors facing lower fixed attention costs for trading, or by the ANcerno data not identifying investors with enough precision. Indeed, the data identify only the management company (e.g., the fund family), and hence some fund families—Fidelity as an extreme example—appear to trade almost continuously over the sample period.
A natural question that arises is whether investors eventually execute the trades that they missed while distracted, that is, whether they “catch up” on their trading. To answer this question, in Figure3, we plot investors’ abnormal trading activity in event time. We use the trading measures that produce the most reliable results in TableII: log(#investors) for retail investors and log($volume) for institutions. In neither case do we find any evidence of abnormal trading in the five trading days that precede or follow a distracting event; the only significant effect occurs on the distraction day itself. Thus, the figure confirms that our distraction events cause shocks to investors’ trading activity that are short-lived. The figure also suggests that the trades forgone on distraction days are superfluous in the sense that they do not lead investors to trade more once the distraction subsides. Such trades are more likely to be speculative, presumably executed by sensation-seeking investors, than liquidity trades motivated by a surplus or shortage of cash. In the next subsection, we provide further evidence consistent with this interpretation.
Distraction effect on retail and institutional trading in event time. This graph shows the coefficient estimates and their confidence intervals from regressing measures of (de-seasonalized and de-trended) retail and institutional trading on a set of event-time dummies. In Panel A, the dependent variable is the logarithm of number of households trading in the retail brokerage data. In Panel B, the dependent variable is the logarithm of the aggregated trade volume in the institutional trade data. In both panels, the bar at 0 represents the coefficient estimate on a dummy variable indicating distraction days; the bar at –1 is the coefficient estimate on a dummy variable indicating trading days one day prior to distraction days. Confidence bars are based on standard errors adjusted for autocorrelation up to three lags with the Newey-West method. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
Having established that investors trade less on distraction days, we now seek to identify which investor types are more prone to distraction. To do so, we sort investors on the extent to which they are biased and/or make losses (or forgo profits) from trading.
Motivated by prior research, we employ seven proxies for investor biasedness. The first proxy, gender, is motivated by Barber and Odean's (2001a) finding that men trade more frequently than women but earn lower returns. This pattern can be attributed to overconfidence (many studies in psychology demonstrate that men are more overconfident than women) or to the pleasure derived from trading (the excitement of gambling; see Dorn and Sengmueller (2009), Grinblatt and Keloharju (2009), Gao and Lin (2014)). We define the dummy variablesingle-maleas an indicator equal to 1 for a single male investor or 0 for a single female investor; we focus on single households because they are likely to display starker differences (Barber and Odean (2001a)).
The second proxy is a dummy variable, indicating whether investors have switched from phone-based to online trading. Prior research shows that online investors are more overconfident (Barber and Odean (2002)). This may be because overconfidence makes investors switch to online trading or because the vast amount of investment information available on the internet provides an illusion of knowledge (Barber and Odean (2001b)). Information on gender and online trading status is available in the retail data only.
Our third proxy is portfolio concentration, which we capture using the average Herfindahl index over monthly portfolio holdings for retail investors and over monthly trading intensities (i.e., trading volumes across stocks) for institutions (since we do not observe their portfolio holdings). An investor who holds a concentrated portfolio forgoes the benefits of diversification, which indicates that either she has strong (possibly ill-placed) faith in her few selected stocks or she is seeking highly skewed (lottery-like) portfolio returns.
Our fourth proxy is trading intensity, which is measured as an investor's average trading volume over the sample period. This metric builds on past evidence that investors trade too much (Barber and Odean (2000)), Kelley and Tetlock (2013)). Our fifth proxy is investment performance as measured by portfolio losses for individuals (the average portfolio return times minus one) or trading losses for institutions (the average post-trade return over the 12 months following a trade times minus one). The rationale here is that investors who are more overconfident or more sensation-seeking should perform worse. For our sixth proxy, we follow Goetzmann and Kumar (2008) and combine portfolio turnover and performance to capture the notion that overconfident or sensation-seeking investors underperform because they trade too much. Specifically, we multiply an investor's portfolio turnover rank by their losses rank. A higher product corresponds to more overconfident or sensation-seeking investors.
Finally, we conjecture that investors with a habit of buying attention-grabbing stocks are more prone to distraction. To capture this propensity, for each investor we calculate the fraction of buy decisions that occur on days when the purchased stock is covered in the media (specifically, in at least one of the four main U.S. newspapers:New York Times,Wall Street Journal,Washington Post, andUSA Today). We argue that investors who react strongly to such (stale) newspaper coverage are also more attracted to sensational news stories and might therefore be more distracted. Previous studies document that the media is positively associated with noise trading (Tetlock (2007), Peress (2014)), and negatively associated with trading performance (Fang, Peress, and Zheng (2014)).
For each of these proxies, we split our sample of investors into halves and then carry out the event study separately on each subsample. We conduct the analysis on both the individual and the institutional transactions data sets while focusing on the most reliable respective trade measures: log(#investors) for individuals and log($volume) for institutions. The results are reported in TableIIIand reveal that, regardless of which proxy is used, investors classified as more biased—be they individuals or institutions—trade less on sensational news days. The difference across groups is remarkable: In 12 out of 12 cases, the distraction effect is larger in the top group than in the bottom group,24with the difference significant at the 10% level in five cases. The difference is also economically sizable: for eight of 12 proxies, the distraction effect in the top group is more than twice that in the bottom group.
Taken together, the results suggest that the sensational news events studied in this paper primarily affect noise traders, that is, sensation-seeking, overconfident, or media-sensitive retail, and institutional investors are more likely to be distracted from trading. It is ironic that, to the extent these investors trade too much because of their overconfidence (Barber and Odean (2001a)), they actuallybenefitfrom being distracted. This finding offers a more nuanced view of inattention. In particular, it suggests that inattention need not be detrimental, as is commonly assumed in the literature—when inattention interacts with behavioral biases, it may actually benefit the trader.
Our results so far indicate that both retail and institutional noise traders are prone to distraction. How strongly distraction affects the overall populations of retail and institutional investors thus depends on the proportion of noise traders within each group.
TableII, which reports that the decline in trading volume is one-third larger for retail than for institutional investors, suggests that distractible noise traders are more prominent among retail investors. The difference between the two investor types might be even larger since prior evidence suggests that the ANcerno data set underrepresents skilled institutions. For instance, Jame (2017) and Choi, Pearson, and Sandy (2016) document no outperformance for the average hedge fund in ANcerno, whereas there is evidence of skill in the broader population (Kosowski, Naik, and Teo (2007); for a survey, see Agarwal, Mullally, and Naik (2015)). To the extent that ANcerno investors are less skilled than the average institution, they may also be more prone to distraction (Fang, Peress, and Zheng (2014)).
Our next analysis presents additional evidence that retail investors are more susceptible to distraction than are institutional investors. Specifically, we conduct an event study using TAQ data for all transactions involving stocks listed on NYSE/AMEX/Nasdaq from 1991 to 2000. The advantage of these data is that they cover all transactions and as a result offer a broad view of the market. A shortcoming is that they do not include traders' identities. However, prior research shows that, until decimalization in 2001, small trades were most likely to be executed by retail investors and large trades by institutions (Hvidkjaer (2008), Barber, Odean, and Zhu (2009)). Accordingly, we investigate how distraction effects vary with trade size.
Panel C of TableIIreports results for the 105 distraction events that occurred during the period 1991–2000. Our measure of trading intensity, log($volume), is the (log) value of trades aggregated separately over small and large trades. On a distraction day, trading volume due to small trades declines by 2% (p< 0.1), whereas the volume due to large trades declines by only a statistically insignificant 0.7%. Column (3) shows that the difference in distraction effects between small and large trades is significant. This suggests that distractible noise traders account for a larger share of retail trading (as proxied by small trade volume) than of institutional trading (as proxied by large trade volume). Thus, in the stock market analysis below, we expect distraction effects to be more pronounced for stocks with high retail ownership.
article.IV. Distraction and the Market:
Having established that noise traders trade less on distraction days, we now investigate the implications for the stock market. To do so, we consider an extension of the Kyle (1985) model; see Internet Appendix SectionIfor details. Specifically, building on a static model based on adverse selection, we introduce inventory concerns by making market makers risk-averse. The resulting model captures two important sources of illiquidity identified in the microstructure literature: adverse selection (Glosten and Milgrom (1985), Kyle (1985)) and inventory risk (Stoll (1978), Ho and Stoll (1980,1981), Grossman and Miller (1988)).
Our model predicts that if noise traders are distracted (as modeled by a reduction in the variance of noise trading), then trading volume declines, liquidity worsens (higher Kyle's lambda), returns become less volatile, and the autocovariance of returns grows (is less negative).
The intuition for these predictions is as follows. Trading volume declines not only because there are fewer noise trades, but also because speculators, who try to conceal their information, scale their trades back.
Liquidity is affected by two opposing forces. On the one hand, a lower variance of noise trades implies that market makers face higher adverse selection risk, which induces them to decrease liquidity. On the other hand, a lower variance reduces inventory risk, which allows market makers to charge a lower risk premium and thus improve liquidity. It turns out that the former effect outweighs the latter, so that a reduction in the variance of noise trades unambiguously leads to reduced liquidity. This is because, in our model, noise shocks affect the amount of inventory that market makers need to absorb but donotaffect the difficulty involved in unwinding this inventory going forward. In this respect, our approach differs from classic models of inventory risk (Ho and Stoll (1980,1981), Grossman and Miller (1988), Hendershott and Menkveld (2014)), where market makers are concerned about fluctuations in the value of this inventory caused byfuturenoise trading.25It is precisely this feature that makes our model well suited for deriving implications of short-lived distraction shocks such as those studied in this paper.
Finally, return volatility and autocovariance are driven by the inventory risk component of liquidity because that component leads to a transient price impact.26Less noise trading means fewer temporary shocks to prices, which reduces volatility and price reversals. Treating our distraction events as a natural experiment, we now set out to test these predictions.
We start with a brief description of the variables used in this section.27We winsorize the data at the 0.5% level in both tails and then purge them of seasonal patterns (as described previously). Throughout our analysis, we focus on equally weighted averages across stocks. Value-weighted averages yield weaker results, which suggests that our findings are concentrated among smaller stocks (we verify this later). To assess the effect of distraction events on stock market performance, we examine the (equal-weighted) average market return on all stocks in CRSP (denotedmkt return). For trading activity, we look at both the average of daily (log) turnover (log(turnover)), defined as the number of shares traded in a stock on a given day divided by the number of shares outstanding,28and the (log) aggregate dollar volume (log($volume)).
We use several measures of liquidity, which are broadly classified into three groups. The first group contains measures that reflect both adverse selection and inventory risk. It includes quoted bid-ask spreads (closing bid-ask spreadfrom CRSP, available as of November 1982, and dailyaverage bid-ask spreadfrom TAQ, both measured relative to the mid-quote) as well aseffective spreadsfrom TAQ (i.e., the percentage increase in the ratio of the transaction price over the prevailing mid-quote prior to the transaction).29
The second group consists of four measures of adverse selection. The first is the Amihud (2002) illiquidity ratio, defined as a stock's (log) absolute return divided by dollar volume.30This measure, denoted log(amihud), is computed from CRSP data and thus is available for the entire sample period. Goyenko, Holden, and Trzcinka (2009) show that it does a good job of capturing adverse selection. The second measure isprice impact, defined as the percentage change in the mid-quote from one second before to five minutes after the transaction. The third measure is theabsolute trade imbalance. Easley et al. (2008), and Kaul, Lei, and Stoffman (2008) argue that this measure captures the intuition of the PIN introduced by Easley, Hvidkjaer, and O'Hara (2002) while having the advantage of being computable at the daily frequency. Our fourth adverse selection measure,lambda, is the slope coefficient from a regression of stock returns on signed order flow over five-minute intervals. It can be interpreted as the cost of demanding a certain amount of liquidity over five minutes. The last three of these measures all require high-frequency TAQ data, so they are available for only a subset of our distraction events.
The third group of our liquidity measures captures noninformational sources of illiquidity, such as inventory or order-handling costs. This group comprisesrealized spread, which is the part of the effective spread that accrues to market makers as compensation for providing liquidity.
Finally, we investigate the impact of distraction on three measures of return volatility and on return autocovariance. Two of the volatility measures—the average stock-level absolute return (abs return) and the logarithm of the ratio of daily high and low prices (price range)—are computed from CRSP data and thus are available for our full sample period. The remaining two measures are the intraday standard deviation of stock returns (intraday volatility) and the intraday autocovariance of stock returns (intraday auto-covariance), both calculated from intraday returns obtained from TAQ.
Descriptive statistics for all stock market variables are reported in Internet AppendixIIA. The magnitudes are in line with previous literature (e.g., Goyenko, Holden, and Trzcinka (2009)) and, after our filters and data adjustments, the measures are reasonably close to a normal distribution, justifying the use of the parametric BMP test in our inferences.
TableIVreports results for the market wide event study. We first note that distraction days have no discernable effects on market returns. This is reassuring since any effect on returns would cast doubt on the presumed noneconomic nature of these events. Other results are relatively weak. For example, dollar volume and turnover decline by 1.1% and 0.8%, respectively, witht-statistics of −1.5 and −1.1. We uncover some significant increases—in closing bid-ask spreads, Amihud illiquidity ratio, and lambda—that are suggestive of an increase in adverse selection risk on distraction days. That being said, most of our measures show insignificant changes for the average stock in our sample. We therefore turn our attention to stocks for which we expect stronger distraction effects, that is, stocks for which noise trading is more pronounced—stocks predominantly held by retail investors (see SectionIII.C).
We sort stocks into terciles based on three common proxies for retail ownership: firm size (market capitalization), the firm's stock price, and the fraction of institutional ownership (drawn from firms’ 13(f) filings). For brevity, we only report the sample split results for firm size—those for stock price and institutional ownership are summarized below and relegated to Internet Appendix SectionsII.BandII.C, respectively.
It is well documented that small stocks are held proportionately more by retail noise traders, so we expect results to be strongest for the bottom tercile in terms of firm size. As Panel A of TableVreports, this is indeed what we find: In the lowest tercile, turnover is significantly reduced (by 2.4%) on distraction days. There is no such effect for stocks in the top tercile, and thedifferencebetween the highest and lowest terciles is strongly significant. A similar pattern emerges for trading volume.
Small stocks also experience a drop in volatility: both the price range and intraday volatility are reduced by a significant 2–3% on distraction days (relative to their unconditional mean: see TableIA.IIin the Internet Appendix). For the largest firms, volatility is unchanged (except for the price range, which exhibits a significant increase according to the parametric BMP test but not according to the nonparametric rank test). The difference between the bottom and top terciles is highly statistically significant. For absolute returns, a similar but statistically insignificant pattern is observed. The return autocovariance increases significantly for small firms, but is unchanged for large ones, implying that small firms have less inefficient (i.e., less negatively autocorrelated) prices on distraction days.
Panel B of TableVreports our findings for liquidity. We find that all measures display a significant increase for small stocks (with the exception of price impact, where the increase is only marginally significant) but are unchanged for large stocks. The difference is significant (again with the exception of price impact). In terms of economic magnitudes, the largest effect is observed for lambda, the regression-based price impact measure. Among small stocks, this measure increases by 11% relative to its unconditional mean, which indicates that demanding liquidity has become substantially more costly. For small stocks, the increase in our spread measures ranges from 2.4% (for average quoted spreads) to 3.8% (for realized spreads). The Amihud illiquidity ratio and the absolute trade imbalance rise by 2.4% and 1.1%, respectively.
In Internet Appendix SectionsII.BandII.C, we sort stocks based on price and institutional ownership derived from 13(f) filings. Low-priced stocks and stocks with low institutional ownership are predominantly held by retail investors (e.g., Brandt et al. (2010)). We therefore expect—and find—stronger results for such stocks: In the lowest terciles in terms of price (TableIA.III) and institutional ownership (TableIA.IV), we find a reduction in trading activity, volatility, and liquidity, as well as an increase in return autocovariance. These changes are typically statistically significant and tend to abate monotonically in the other terciles. For most measures, the difference between the top and bottom terciles is also significant.
Taken together, our results are in line with what our model predicts when noise traders are distracted (see SectionIV.Aand Internet Appendix SectionI). The increase in realized spreads further suggests that, for small stocks, the provision of liquidity—in addition to the demand for those stocks—is reduced. We return to this observation in SectionV.D.
The results from our three sample splits paint a consistent picture: on distraction days, stocks with high levels of retail ownership experience a reduction in trading activity, volatility, price reversals, and liquidity, whereas stocks with low retail ownership are unaffected. Our model demonstrates that these findings can all be attributed to a common shock, namely, a reduction in noise trading. This interpretation further implies that distraction events that display a larger drop in trading should exhibit a more pronounced reduction in volatility, price reversals, and liquidity.
In this subsection, we test this prediction by regressing abnormal volatility, return autocovariance, and liquidity on abnormal trading activity in the cross-section of distraction events. The results are reported in TableVI. For all measures of volatility, we find a strong positive relation to abnormal turnover (from CRSP) in the subsample of firms in the bottom size tercile. This result suggests that distraction events with a larger drop (i.e., more negative) in turnover experience a larger drop (i.e., more negative) in volatility as well. Similarly, we obtain statistically negative coefficients when we regress liquidity measures on turnover, which suggests that events with a larger reduction in trading see a larger increase in spreads/illiquidity. Replacing share turnover with small TAQ trades, the number of retail traders from the brokerage data set or institutional trading volume from the Ancerno data set yields similar but statistically weaker results, owing to the smaller number of observations for these shorter time series (see TableIA.XVIin the Internet Appendix).
Overall, the results above establish that our findings for trading activity, volatility, price reversals, and liquidity are all interconnected. The results therefore strengthen our interpretation, derived from the model, that all of these effects are caused by a reduction in noise trading.
article.V. Robustness, Endogeneity, and Alternat:
In this section, we check the robustness of our results to both the choice of keywords used to construct the event list and event clustering. In addition, we address endogeneity concerns and we discuss alternative explanations related to distracting agents other than noise traders and to investor sentiment.
As described in SectionII, we filter the initial set of 1,108 high-news pressure days down to 551 sample event-days using a list of economic keywords designed to address the possible confounding effects of news about the economy. In unreported analyses, we find that our results are robust to changes in the event list. For example, we check that our results are not driven by a few outlier events. We also experiment with a manual filter, since some events may have economic relevance even though no economic keyword appears in the headline (e.g., political party conventions, where economic policy agendas are announced and presidential candidates are determined). Results are very similar to those obtained with the arguably less subjective keyword filter.
The fact that specific keywords do not alter our results leads us to conclude that, despite their differences, most high-news pressure days are much alike: they feature sensational news stories that have little bearing on the economy. This inference is confirmed in TableIA.VIIof the Internet Appendix, which uses all 1,108 high-news pressure days and reports even stronger results (due to the increase in statistical power resulting from doubling the number of events; the only exception is the return autocovariance, which yields slightly weaker results). Both trading activity and volatility decline significantly in the bottom terciles of firm size, stock price, and institutional ownership. All adverse selection measures increase significantly, including price impact, which was only marginally significant before. The other spread measures also increase.
Given that some distraction events are separated by only a few days, the clustering of events over time may have lead us to overstate the statistical significance of our findings. According to TableI, for example, the two most newsworthy days in 1986 (January 28 and 29) each concerned the Challenger space shuttle explosion. Thus far, we treat such events as independent. In TableIA.VIIof the Internet Appendix, we repeat our main analyses after restricting the sample todistinctdistraction events, defined as events that occur at least five business days (one full calendar week) apart. This approach is conservative because it discards all of the information contained in an event that is close to a previous one. Although only 382 events survive this requirement, our main results continue to hold: stocks with high retail ownership (as proxied by firm size, stock price, or institutional ownership) experience a significant decrease in trading activity, volatility, and liquidity, and an increase in return autocovariance. We therefore conclude that our findings are robust to event clustering.
News pressure—the criterion that we use to identify candidate distraction events—could be endogenous to the stock market for two reasons.
The first endogeneity concern is that news pressure may be elevated on days with importanteconomicnews. In that case, the patterns we document for the market may be caused by the economic news itself—rather than by investor distraction, as we claim. The economic filters that we impose attempt to mitigate this concern. In addition, we show that news pressure is only weakly related to indicators of business activity, newspaper sentiment, or macroeconomic news releases (see Internet AppendixIII.B), and that our event-study results are virtually unchanged after removing stocks that operate in sectors that might be affected by certain types of events (e.g., firms in construction or insurance for events involving natural disasters and firms in regulated industries for political events; see Internet AppendixII.G). Notwithstanding, it is impossible to guarantee that none of the events on our list affect the U.S. economy. We emphasize, however, that this concern is inconsistent with two aspects of our results. First, we find that only small, low-priced, and low-institutional ownership stocks are affected. This observation does not fit with the well-established observation that large stocks lead smaller ones in terms of price discovery (e.g., Lo and MacKinlay (1990), McQueen, Pinegar, and Thorley (1996))—if our distraction events were about economic news, one would expect large stocks to respond first. Second and more importantly, economic news should bias our resultsagainstfinding a distraction effect because economic news triggers more rather than less turnover and volatility.31In essence, our identification strategy draws on the discrepancy between the exuberantnews coverageand the fundamentalnewsworthinessof a media story. Distraction effects such as those that we document prevail when the former outweighs the latter.
The second endogeneity concern is reverse causality: news pressure may be especially high on days withlittleeconomic news. Indeed, TV news broadcasts may devote considerable time to economically irrelevant stories precisely because they have nothing newsworthy to report about the economy. This explanation is consistent with our finding that trading activity and volatility are low when news pressure is high. We make four counterarguments. First, if high news pressure is caused by the absence of newsworthy material on high-news pressure days, then one would expect to find lower TV viewership on those days. This explanation is difficult to reconcile with the surge in TV viewership documented in TableI. Second, by excluding economic news days from the estimation window, our event-study approach ensures that we compare high-news pressure days without economic news to other days without economic news. This means that our results cannot be driven by an implicit sorting on the absence of (macro-)economic news. Third, using different proxies for the amount of firm-specific news, we find no evidence that our distraction days exhibit fewer (or more) firm-specific news than other days (see TableIA.XVof the Internet Appendix). Fourth, we conduct a placebo analysis onlow-news pressure days, where events consist of days with no economic news (as in the main analysis) but on which news pressure is in the lowest decile for the year (rather than in the highest decile, as in the main analysis). If the reverse causality argument were correct, days with low-news pressure would feature lots of economic news, and we should expect these days to display heightened trading activity and volatility. The results of this placebo exercise, reported in TableIA.IXof the Internet Appendix, indicate no such effect. In short, our results are driven by positive spikes in news pressure unrelated to the economy, that is, by distracting events.
In this subsection, we evaluate whether our results can be explained by agents other than noise traders—namely, informed speculators or market makers—being distracted. Using our model in Internet Appendix SectionI, we find that distracting either of these two types of agents yields predictions that do not match the facts. In particular, when speculators are distracted (as modeled by a decrease in their signal precision), liquidity should increase—not decrease as we find. This is because speculators trade less aggressively when they are less well informed, which reduces adverse selection risk and hence prompts market makers to improve liquidity.32
In contrast, when only market makers are distracted (as modeled by a decrease in their signal precision), one expects returns to become more volatile and their autocovariance to decline—again the opposite of what we find. Intuitively, market makers’ signal represents their interpretation of public information. As their signal becomes less precise, market makers assign more weight to the information conveyed by the order flow. This leads to greater price impact, which magnifies the return volatility. Likewise, the return autocovariance becomes more negative, that is, prices reverse more.
In sum, alternative explanations based on insider or market maker distraction are unable to jointly explain our findings for trading volume, volatility, and liquidity. These results reinforce the message that emerges from the trade-level analysis in SectionIII: the sensational news events studied in our paper primarily distract noise traders.33
Many of the distraction events in our sample carry a negative connotation because they pertain to natural disasters, terrorist attacks, accidents, or celebrity deaths. One may thus wonder whether our results could be explained (or confounded) by shocks to investor sentiment.34In our view, there are three reasons why this is unlikely. First, a negative shock to sentiment should be associated with a significantly negative return. Although we do observe a negative sign for the abnormal market return on distraction days (see TableIV), this effect is both economically small and statistically insignificant. Second, Garcia (2013) reports that both positive and negative shocks to investor sentiment lead to a surge in trading activity—yet we observe the exact opposite on distraction days. Finally, we manually split our distraction sample into positive, neutral, and negative events but fail to find a significantly lower return for the negative events (see Internet AppendixIII.H). We conclude that the sensational news stories in our sample are at most remotely related to investor sentiment.
article.VI. Distracted Noise Traders and Technol:
Our distraction events span more than four decades—a period that has seen profound changes in both the composition of the investor population and the trading environment. In this section, we first examine how distraction effects have evolved over time. We then examine how these effects were impacted by two major developments: the Internet and algorithmic trading.
We conduct the event study separately over the three subperiods 1968–1992, 1993–2006, and 2007–2014. The second period captures the influence of the Internet, which revolutionized investment practices throughout the 1990s by facilitating access to information and enabling online trading. The start of the Internet era is hard to pin down precisely. The first web browser (written by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN) was released to the public in 1991. The hugely popular web browser Netscape and the search engine Yahoo! were created in 1994. The year 1993 thus seems a reasonable start date—it is also a convenient one as TAQ data start in 1993. The third period corresponds to one in which algorithmic traders—and chief among them, high-frequency traders—have come to dominate trading. According to a report by the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) (2010), these traders accounted for more than half of the trading volume in U.S. equity markets in 2009. Deregulation (Regulation National Market System (NMS), passed in 2005 and implemented in 2007) and technological advances (e.g., ultra-fast communication lines) have driven the growth of algorithmic trading.
The results of the subperiod analysis are reported in TableVII. As we move from the first to the second subperiod, the impact of distraction is substantially greater for virtually all variables. For example, the effect grows from −1.4% to −3.2% for turnover, and from –3.4% to –6.6% for the price range; the closing bid-ask spread, in relative terms, increases from 1.7% (= 0.074%/4.23%) to 3.1% (= 0.075%/2.54%, where 4.23% and 2.54% are the average spreads over the first and second subperiods, respectively). The change from the second to the third subperiod reveals more contrasting patterns across variables. For trading activity, volatility, and price reversals, the impact of distraction is further amplified in the later period. For example, the effect grows from −3.2% to −4.3% for turnover, and similar increases are observed for return autocovariance and volatility. In contrast, for liquidity, the effect vanishes altogether: in the most recent period, none of the liquidity variables worsens significantly on distraction days.35
It is worth noting that distraction effects have increased throughout the sample period (with the exception of liquidity in the later period) in spite of a secular decline in retail trading. Indeed, retail investors, who directly owned 57% of the stock market in 1991, saw their share slide to 37% by 2007 (Rydqvist, Spizman, and Strebulaev (2014)), with a concomitant decline in their share of trading (Chordia, Roll, and Subrahmanyam (2011)).36The persistence of distraction effects is consistent with our finding that institutional noise traders are distracted as well (see SectionIII).
In the following two subsections, we attempt to make sense of the time patterns of distraction effects, starting with the influence of the Internet.
We find that distraction effects increased with the advent of the Internet. Previous work suggests that, rather than being a mere coincidence, the Internet may have caused this amplification through two distinct channels. The first is that the Internet reinforces investors’ overconfidence. The vast amount of investment information that it offers gives investors anillusion of knowledge, leading them to overestimate their stock-picking ability (Barber and Odean (2001b)). Moreover, by enabling direct trading online, it increases investors’ sense of control over the outcome of their trades. Consistent with an increase in overconfidence, Barber and Odean (2002) document that households trade more actively and less profitably after switching from phone-based to online trading (see also Choi, Laibson, and Metrick (2002), for evidence from 401(k) accounts). The second channel is the availability of timely information. Access to real-time data about the market, as well as the ability to execute orders rapidly and check portfolios several times a day, allow investors to respond to news more quickly. As a result, distraction effects are less protracted over time and thus appear amplified in the data.
The evidence in TableIIIis consistent with both channels. Specifically, the table shows that households that trade online are almost twice as likely to be distracted as those that do not. To shed further light on the impact of the Internet, we compare tech to nontech stocks. We posit that, especially in the early days of the Internet, tech stocks were more likely to be held by tech-savvy investors, that is, early adopters of online trading. In line with this hypothesis, we find in our brokerage data set that tech stocks have a 24% larger probability of being traded online (z-statistic = 4.9). We therefore expect tech stocks to exhibit stronger distraction effects. TableVIIIpresents event-study results after classifying stocks in the bottom size tercile—stocks that display significant distraction effects—as tech versus nontech based on SIC codes as in Loughran and Ritter (2004). The patterns in distraction effects that we observe across tech and nontech stocks mirror those that we find across the pre- and post-Internet eras: distraction effects for trading activity, volatility, and liquidity are all greater for tech stocks. This finding suggests that the Internet, by vastly increasing the amount and velocity of information available to investors and by speeding up their trading responses, is responsible for the amplification of distraction effects observed during the 1990s.
As we proceed from the second to the third period, distraction effects display a striking divergence: they strengthen for trading activity, volatility, and price reversals, but they disappear for liquidity. Making sense of this divergence is not straightforward. For example, it might be rationalized by insiders, since 2007, being subject to distraction just like noise traders,37but this explanation strikes us as implausible. If anything, technology has made insiders more, rather than less, attentive over the post-2007 period (e.g., Chakrabarty Moulton, and Wang (2018)). Alternatively, one might argue that inventory concerns have become more severe since 2007. Since the adverse selection and inventory risk channels have opposing effects on liquidity, a strengthening of the latter would explain why the distraction effect has dampened for liquidity. However, this explanation is difficult to square with the fact that liquidity has improved over the same period (e.g., Hendershott, Jones, and Menkveld (2011), Angel, Harris, and Spatt (2013)), as greater inventory concerns would seem to imply less rather than more liquidity.
The above discussion leads us to speculate that the post-2007 patterns may result from noise tradersinteractingwith algorithmic traders. Indeed, technological advances—improvements in both hardware (e.g., computing power, custom-designed chips, and ultra-fast communication lines) and software (e.g., pattern recognition algorithms, “Big Data,” and artificial intelligence)—and new business practices (e.g., colocation, access to exchanges’ proprietary data feeds) have made it easier to detect and anticipate noise trades. These changes have contributed to the development of two types of algorithmic trading.38
The first,agency algorithms, are used by buy-side institutions to minimize the price impact of their trades, given a target portfolio. An example of such investors are the activist-shareholders analyzed by Collin-Dufresne and Fos (2015). These shareholders strategically time their informed trades (later disclosed in their SEC Schedule 13D filings) to occur when they anticipate more active noise trading, such as when volume on the S&P 500 market index is abnormally high (see also Dong, Feng, and Sadka (2017)). With the development of these agency algorithms, the decline in noise trading on days of sensational news prompts a more severe reduction in informed trades. The consequence for volume and volatility is an amplification during the later subperiod (since informed trades generate volatility over the short run). For liquidity, in contrast, the consequence is an attenuation because there is less adverse selection. This attenuation is consistent with the empirical evidence presented in Collin-Dufresne and Fos (2015) and Kacperczyk and Pagnotta (2019), who document that adverse selection measures fail to detect the presence of informed trading in the stock market.
The second type of algorithmic trading,high-frequency trading, is characterized by the speed (on the order of milliseconds or faster) with which orders are placed and modified. It has been argued that high-frequency traders take advantage of slow traders by front-running their orders, with noise traders presumed to be especially slow. Indeed, high-frequency trading firms pay retail brokers hundreds of millions of dollars each year for the right to execute their customers’ orders—a practice that enables front-running and has drawn scrutiny from the SEC.39We speculate that front-running by high-frequency traders (i.e., trading on short-run price changes predicted by information about incoming order flow) has a multiplier effect on trading volume. Hence, when noise trading declines on distraction days, trading volume goes down by more than the forgone noise trades because high-frequency traders scale back their front-running trades.40The distraction effect on liquidity may simultaneously be dampened to the extent that high-frequency liquidity providers are quick to adapt to incoming informed orders, thus alleviating adverse selection. Consistent with this conjecture, Van Kervel and Menkveld (2019) document that high-frequency traders rapidly switch from supplying liquidity to front-running when trading alongside informed institutional orders—a practice the authors call “with-wind trading.”
These considerations prompt us to investigate how noise trading interacts with algorithmic trading in the post-2007 period. To this end, we examine distraction effects for stocks—again belonging to the bottom size tercile—sorted on the intensity of algorithmic trading. We measure this intensity using three proxies, each based on a distinct data source. The first proxy, based on TAQ data, is the quote-to-trade ratio, or the number of quote updates divided by the number of trades.41A higher ratio indicates more algorithmic trading (see Hendershott, Jones, and Menkveld (2011), Conrad, Wahal, and Xiang (2015), Brogaard, Hendershott, and Riordan (2017)). The second proxy uses (back-filled) data from the SEC's Market Information Data Analytics System (MIDAS) to construct an index based on four variables that are associated with algorithmic trading (Weller (2017)).42The third proxy is the stock price, orthogonalized with respect to firm size (i.e., the residual from regressing stock price on the logarithm of market capitalization). Weller (2017) shows that this measure is a valid instrument for algorithmic trading activity—because of the minimum tick size of one cent imposed by SEC Rule 612 (the “sub-penny” rule), high-price stocks have a finer price grid, which advantages algorithms over humans for continually updating quotes.
TableIXpresents event study results for the quote-to-trade ratio. Findings based on MIDAS data and the orthogonalized price are similar and relegated to Internet Appendix SectionsII.IandII.J, respectively. We find that for the 87 distraction events occurring in the post-2007 period, the distraction effects for trading activity, volatility, and price reversals are more pronounced for stocks with more algorithmic trading (i.e., stocks in the top tercile). The differences across terciles are statistically significant for all but price reversals. In contrast, the distraction effects for liquidity tend to dampen among high-algorithmic trading stocks: the difference across terciles is negative for seven out of eight liquidity measures, significantly so for two. These findings suggest that the heightened distraction effects for volume, volatility, and price reversals and the attenuated liquidity effect that we find for the post-2007 period (see TableVII) are driven mainly by stocks characterized by a high level of algorithmic trading.
In short, far from dissipating with the downtrend in retail ownership, distraction effects have strengthened over time. Our evidence suggests that technological advances play a role in this amplification. The arrival of the Internet first rendered noise traders more overconfident and/or reactive to sensational news. Then came “robot” investors, which interact with noise traders in ways that exacerbate their impact on volume, volatility, and return reversals, while muting their impact on liquidity.
article.VII. Conclusion:
This paper studies how the limited attention of noise traders affects the stock market. We exploit episodes of sensational news that are exogenous to the stock market and that distract noise traders among retail and institutional investors to examine how investor distraction affects market outcomes. We find that distraction days are associated with reduced trading activity, volatility, and liquidity among stocks for which noise trading is expected to be more pronounced, that is, stocks with small market capitalization, a low share price, and/or a low level of institutional ownership. We rationalize these findings in a parsimonious model that extends (Kyle (1985)) to incorporate market makers’ concern for inventory risk.
Our findings have important implications for the literature on liquidity. In particular, our findings confirm that liquidity deteriorates in response to short-lived reductions in the intensity of noise trading—as predicted by theories of adverse selection (e.g., Glosten and Milgrom (1985), Kyle (1985))—and provide countervailing evidence to research documenting a liquidity improvement after a permanent reduction in the intensity of retail (noise) trading (Foucault, Sraer, and Thesmar (2011)). We reconcile these seemingly inconsistent results by noting that adverse selection and inventory risk are affected differently depending on the persistence of shocks to the intensity of noise trading: inventory concerns loom large when such shocks are persistent (as in Foucault, Sraer, and Thesmar (2011)), but adverse selection dominates when they are not (as in the case of our distraction shocks).
Our results highlight that the importance of noise trading has not declined in recent years, despite the downward trend in direct retail ownership. We offer three possible explanations for this observation. First, retail noise traders may simply have been replaced by institutional ones. Second, changes to the information and trading environments, such as those caused by the Internet, may have emboldened noise traders and offered new trading stimuli. Third, noise traders may interact with algorithmic traders in ways that magnify their impact. Indeed, we find that distraction effects for trading activity and volatility (but not for liquidity) have become stronger in recent years among stocks with a large presence of algorithmic traders. Since algorithmic traders are unlikely to be distracted themselves, this finding suggests that such interaction effects could be important. We look forward to seeing more work along these lines.
